notes.section_title: Belgium and Sri Lanka: Contrasting Stories

content.text: Belgium is a small European country with a very complex ethnic composition. Of the total population, 59 per cent live in the Flemish region and speak Dutch, while 40 per cent live in the Wallonia region and speak French.

content.title: The Brussels Problem
content.text: In the capital city, Brussels, the situation was reversed: 80 per cent were French-speaking while 20 per cent were Dutch-speaking. The Dutch-speaking majority in the country was a minority in the capital, leading to tensions in the 1950s and 1960s.

Sri Lanka has a diverse population. The major groups are Sinhala-speakers (74 per cent) and Tamil-speakers (18 per cent). Among Tamils, there are 'Sri Lankan Tamils' (natives) and 'Indian Tamils' (plantation workers' descendants).
Sri Lanka achieved independence in 1948. The Sinhala majority sought to establish supremacy through majoritarian measures.

content.title: Majoritarian Measures (1956 Act)
items: Recognized Sinhala as the only official language, disregarding Tamil.
Followed preferential policies for Sinhalas in university positions and government jobs.
Mandated the protection and fostering of Buddhism by the State.

content.title: Consequences
content.text: These measures led to a feeling of alienation among Tamils. Strained relations eventually turned into a violent Civil War, which caused a terrible setback to the country's social and economic life before ending in 2009.
The Belgian leaders chose to respect regional and cultural differences. Between 1970 and 1993, they amended their constitution four times to ensure everyone could live together.

content.title: The Belgian Model
items: Constitution mandates equal number of Dutch and French-speaking ministers in the Central Government.
Many powers of the Central Government were given to State Governments, which are not subordinate to the center.
Brussels has a separate government with equal representation for both communities.
Community Government: Elected by people belonging to one language group; has power over cultural, educational, and language issues.

content.text: Power sharing is beneficial for two distinct sets of reasons: Prudential and Moral.

content.title: Comparison of Reasons
items: Prudential reasons: Focus on better outcomes. It reduces the possibility of social conflict and ensures the stability of political order.
Moral reasons: Focus on the very nature of democracy. Power sharing is the 'spirit' of democracy. It ensures that those affected by governance are consulted and have a stake in the system.

content.title: Institutional Forms
items: Horizontal: Power shared among different organs of government—Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary. This system of 'Checks and Balances' ensures no organ has unlimited power.
Vertical: Power shared at different levels—Central/Union government and State/Provincial governments. This is called the 'Federal Division' of power.

topics.topic_name: Power Sharing among Social & Political Groups

content.title: Diversity and Influence
items: Social Groups: Power shared among religious and linguistic groups (e.g., Community government or reserved constituencies).
Political Forces: Power shared through competition among political parties, leading to coalition governments and the influence of interest groups/movements on policy-making.

content.text: In Beirut, Lebanon, power is shared based on a fixed communal pact: President (Catholic Christian), PM (Sunni Muslim), Deputy PM (Orthodox Christian), and Speaker (Shi'a Muslim).

content.title: The Conflict
content.text: While this pact ended the civil war, individuals like Khalil, who do not want to be identified by their parents' religion, find it restrictive. It highlights the tension between communal representation and individual merit-based democracy.

glossary.definition: A social division based on shared culture and common descent, though members need not have the same religion or nationality.
A belief that the majority community should be able to rule a country in whichever way it wants, disregarding the wishes of the minority.
A violent conflict between opposing groups within a country that becomes so intense that it appears like a war.
Decisions based on careful calculation of gains and losses, often contrasted with decisions based purely on moral considerations.
Distribution of power among organs at the same level (Legislature, Executive, Judiciary), ensuring a system of checks and balances.
An arrangement where each organ of the government checks the others, resulting in a balance of power among various institutions.
A government elected by people belonging to one language community, having powers over cultural and educational issues (e.g., in Belgium).
A government formed by two or more political parties together, usually when no single party wins a clear majority.

summary_points: Belgium and Sri Lanka demonstrate that the success of a democracy depends on how it handles ethnic and linguistic diversity.
Majoritarianism in Sri Lanka led to alienation and civil war, while accommodation in Belgium preserved national unity through power-sharing.
Power-sharing is desirable for 'prudential' reasons (maintaining stability) and 'moral' reasons (respecting the spirit of democracy).
Horizontal power-sharing among government organs creates a system of checks and balances, ensuring no single organ becomes absolute.
Vertical power-sharing involves a federal structure with central, state, and local governments having defined spheres of authority.
Democracies can also share power among social groups through reserved seats and among political parties through coalitions.
The Belgian model effectively balances national interests with regional and linguistic identities through its innovative community governments.
Khalil's story in Lebanon illustrates that while communal power-sharing can provide peace, it can also conflict with individual democratic aspirations.
